title: Quiz: Quantum Approaches to Graph Isomorphism
description: Test your understanding of graph isomorphism, its complexity, and quantum approaches

questions.question: What is the current best known classical time complexity for graph isomorphism (Babai 2015)?

options.text: O(n^3) - polynomial time
O(2^n) - exponential time
O(exp(log^c n)) - quasipolynomial time
O(n!) - factorial time

questions.explanation: Babai's 2015 breakthrough established that graph isomorphism can be solved in quasipolynomial time O(exp(log^c n)) for some constant c. This is faster than any polynomial but slower than polynomial time. Whether GI is in P remains an open problem.

questions.question: How is graph isomorphism formulated as a hidden subgroup problem (HSP)?

options.text: HSP over the cyclic group Z_n
HSP over the symmetric group S_n x S_n
HSP over the dihedral group D_n
HSP over the general linear group GL(n)

questions.explanation: Graph isomorphism can be formulated as an HSP over S_n x S_n, where the hidden subgroup consists of pairs (pi, pi) where pi is an automorphism of the first graph (if isomorphic) or related permutations. This formulation connects GI to quantum algorithms but doesn't immediately yield a speedup.

questions.question: Why does the standard quantum Fourier transform approach fail to efficiently solve graph isomorphism?

options.text: The QFT cannot be applied to graphs
Graph isomorphism is not a decision problem
Irreducible representations of S_n can have exponentially large dimension
The hidden subgroup is always trivial

questions.explanation: The irreducible representations of the symmetric group S_n can have dimension up to exp(sqrt(n)). After applying the QFT, measuring in the representation basis collapses information needed to identify the hidden subgroup. This is in contrast to abelian groups where all irreps are 1-dimensional.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a graph invariant?

options.text: Number of vertices
Degree sequence
Vertex labels
Eigenvalues of the adjacency matrix

questions.explanation: Vertex labels are not an invariant because relabeling vertices (applying a permutation) changes the labels but preserves isomorphism. Invariants must have the same value for isomorphic graphs regardless of labeling. Number of vertices, degree sequence, and adjacency spectrum are all invariants.

questions.question: Two graphs with the same adjacency matrix eigenvalues (cospectral graphs) are:

options.text: Always isomorphic
Never isomorphic
Not necessarily isomorphic - the spectrum is not a complete invariant
Isomorphic only if they have the same number of vertices

questions.explanation: The adjacency spectrum is an invariant but not a complete invariant. There exist pairs of non-isomorphic graphs with identical eigenvalues (cospectral graphs). Examples include certain pairs of trees and the famous 'cospectral pair' of 4-regular graphs on 6 vertices.

questions.question: If two graphs G and H are encoded as quantum states |G> and |H>, then G is isomorphic to H if and only if |G> = |H> (up to global phase).

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. When graphs are encoded as uniform superpositions over their automorphism orbits, isomorphic graphs produce identical quantum states (up to global phase), while non-isomorphic graphs produce orthogonal states. This is because the state captures the equivalence class under permutation.

questions.question: What is the automorphism group of a graph?

options.text: The set of all graphs isomorphic to it
The set of permutations that map the graph to itself
The set of all edges in the graph
The eigenspace of the adjacency matrix

questions.explanation: The automorphism group Aut(G) consists of all permutations of vertices that preserve adjacency - that is, permutations pi such that (u,v) is an edge if and only if (pi(u), pi(v)) is an edge. For highly symmetric graphs like the complete graph K_n, Aut(K_n) = S_n.

questions.question: Which of the following are true about the complexity of graph isomorphism? (Select all that apply)

options.text: GI is known to be NP-complete
GI is in NP (verifiable in polynomial time)
No polynomial quantum algorithm for GI is known
GI is at least as hard as factoring integers

questions.explanation: GI is in NP because given a proposed isomorphism, we can verify it in polynomial time by checking that it preserves all edges (b). No polynomial quantum algorithm is known for GI (c). GI is NOT known to be NP-complete and is believed not to be (a is false). GI and factoring are not known to have any reduction relationship (d is false).
